data.title: Mandalay Resources: Greater Visibility For Shareholders In Combination With Alkane Resources
data.description:
A Hold Rating for Shares of Mandalay Resources Corporation

With this analysis, our first for this stock, we would like to say that Mandalay Resources Corporation (OTCQB:MNDJF) deserves a Hold rating, in the sense that portfolios with Mandalay have growth potential and that volatility offers them an incredible opportunity to expand on that potential.

The growth opportunity is described in the following Seeking Alpha news dated April 28, 2025, at 7:28 AM ET:

Alkane Resources to buy Canada's Mandalay Resources in $358M deal

Throughout this discussion, we will highlight why we believe Mandalay shareholders will realise impressive benefits upon completion of the A$559.1 million (≈ US$358 million) all-share transaction between the two companies. Under this transaction, Mandalay shareholders will receive 7.875 ordinary shares of Alkane Resources Ltd (OTCPK:ALKEF). Mandalay and Alkane shareholders will each hold 45% and 55%, respectively, of the total outstanding shares of the new, combined company. Based on the market price of the two stocks at the time of this writing (MNDJF: US$ 3.81/share vs. ALKEF: US$ 0.46/share), Mandalay shareholders would receive 8,283 common shares, which ALKEF must issue in exchange for each MNDJF common share. However, under the agreement, Mandalay shareholders will only receive 7.875 common shares. The market value of the US-listed MNDJF shares is quite high relative to the number of common shares they will receive as part of the all-stock merger agreement. To be clear, Mandalay shareholders will receive the number of newly issued shares from Alkane at a fixed ratio of 7.875:1 for each Mandalay share held, regardless of the share price on the stock market from now until the completion of the merger in Q3 2025. However, if the strategy is to own a larger stake in the newly merged company, we believe it is always better to preserve as much capital as possible while the investor builds up their position in Mandalay, which can then be exchanged for newly issued Alkane shares upon the material execution of the all-share deal transaction. This strategy must consider two dynamics, ultimately shifting from a "hold" stance to a more bullish stance if: A) the exchange ratio trend is equal to or lower than 7.875:1; B) Mandalay shares are undervalued relative to past trends. Investors should therefore wait until the cyclical fluctuations of Mandalay's share price are reflected in both dynamics simultaneously. In terms of the newly issued shares they will receive from Alkane under the all-stock deal transaction, investors would not overpay for Mandalay shares and potentially use a price in line with a "buy-the-dip" approach. Given the volatility of the gold price, it may be possible to temporarily depart from the "Hold" rating and pursue the goal of a greater future participation in the larger, newly merged company through the strength of the position in Mandalay Resources' US-listed stock in the pre-merger phase.

Investors had the opportunity to profit from the "dip" (technically, a significant decline) in Mandalay's share price around May 19. Due to cyclicality, which correlates positively with gold price fluctuations, Mandalay shares traded significantly lower than when the all-stock transaction was announced on April 28, despite the expected benefits of the merger with Alkane.

The positive correlation between Gold Spot Price (XAUUSD:CUR) and the Mandalay Resources share price means that on average the securities move in the same direction (up-up or down-down), regardless of returns, which can also vary widely over a given period, as correlation has nothing to do with the returns the securities can generate. The positive correlation in the chart above is described by a solid yellow curve line that is above zero (in positive territory) for almost the entire observed period.

Strict adherence to the "7.875:1 maximum, plus price dip" strategy requires a disciplined trading strategy that allows investors to mitigate the potential negative consequences of Mandalay Resources' low liquidity stock. According to the trading section of the Seeking Alpha website, the Average Volume (3 months) of publicly traded U.S. shares of Mandalay Resources is 35,794 out of a total of 94.78 million shares outstanding (Ticker).

Mandalay-Alkane Merger: What Changes for Shareholders

The merger of Mandalay and Alkane is expected to create a larger and more efficient company with a stronger presence in jurisdictions favourable for exploration and mining activities, greater visibility in the financial markets, increased stock market popularity, and a significantly better position to benefit from the sharp rise in the price of gold.

In this announcement, Globe Newswire describes both companies.

About Alkane Resources

Alkane Resources intends to grow to become one of Australia's leading multi-mine gold and copper producers. The Company's current gold production is from the Tomingley Gold mine in New South Wales, which has been operating since 2014 and has operating plans extending beyond 2030.

Alkane also owns a major porphyry gold-copper deposit discovered at Boda-Kaiser in 2019. Exploration is ongoing and economics demonstrated in a 2024 scoping study. Alkane's gold interests extend throughout Australia, with strategic investments in other gold exploration and aspiring mining companies.

For existing shareholders and potential investors of Mandalay Resources, the Globe Newswire describes their business as follows:

About Mandalay Resources

Mandalay Resources is a Canadian-based natural resource company with producing assets in Australia (Costerfield gold-antimony mine) and Sweden (Björkdal gold mine). The Company is focused on growing its production and reducing costs to generate significant positive cashflow. Mandalay is committed to operating safely and in an environmentally responsible manner, while developing a high level of community and employee engagement.

Production Expanded, Large Presence in Mining-friendly Jurisdiction

Mandalay produces not only gold (about 80% of total sales), 20,376 ounces were produced in the most recent quarter of Q1-2025, but also antimony, 161 tonnes in Q1 2025, resulting in 25,779 ounces of gold equivalent produced in Q1 2025.

The merger is subject to shareholder approval of both companies and regulatory approval. Upon completion of the merger, Mandalay Resources shareholders will no longer own the targeted production of 85,000 to 95,000 ounces of gold equivalent in 2025 at an All-In Sustaining Cost of US$1,795 to US$1,975 per ounce of gold equivalent, in accordance with the company's full-year 2025 guidance.

Instead, they will be invested in a merged company with a production target of approximately 160,000 ounces of gold equivalent in 2025 and the potential to increase this production to over 180,000 ounces by 2026 at a lower All-In Sustaining Cost per ounce of gold equivalent. The enlarged company is expected to produce the metals in 2025 at a profit margin defined by an All-In-Sustaining Cost ("AISC") of approximately A$2,750 (approximately US$1,760) per ounce. The operation is expected to become so cost-effective that lower AISCs of approximately A$2,160/oz Au Eq. or approximately US$1,420/oz Au Eq. are forecast for the following year, 2026.

The merged company will operate three producing mines: Tomingley (Alkane Resources Limited) and Costerfield (Mandalay Resources Corporation) for gold in Australia, and Björkdal (Mandalay Resources Corporation) for gold and antimony in Sweden, in prime jurisdictions.

The Mining Risk Heatmap from Sprott—a global asset manager with extensive experience in physical investments in metals and minerals, as well as close relationships and expertise in the mining industry—shows that Australia and Sweden have a dark blue code. This means these regions have a very mining and exploration-friendly jurisdiction and environment, resulting in very low, if not any, investment risk.

Diversified Metal Portfolio in Light of the Rosy Outlook

The merger proponents aim to diversify the raw materials portfolio, which will be conducive to future economic development.

Gold Outlook

The new company will produce a larger amount of gold, thereby being better positioned to capitalise on the growing trend that sees the precious metal as a safe haven against the negative winds in a scenario where turbulence will become more and more regular and pose a serious risk to portfolios. As a benchmark for the gold price, the Gold Spot Price is trading at US$ 3,361.09 per troy ounce, which at the time of writing represents an increase of ≈ 28.58% year-to-date and ≈ 45.42% over the past 12 months, mainly due to strong demand for safe-haven assets. In fact, as stated in the Equity Insider News Commentary issued on behalf of RUA GOLD Inc. (RUA:CA) on May 27, 2025, via PR Newswire and reported by Seeking Alpha:

Safe-haven demand, rate cut expectations, and fresh volatility tied to US President Donald Trump's tariff warnings have sent bullion prices soaring, prompting Citi to raise its short-term gold target to $3,500. Bank of America followed by increasing its gold exposure in Q4, citing a bullish shift in underlying market conditions.

[…]

Veteran investor Rob McEwen and billionaire John Paulson both see $5,000 gold within reach, while JPMorgan recently projected a potential path to $6,000 if even a sliver of U.S.-held foreign assets are reallocated toward bullion.

Antimony Outlook

Demand for antimony is strengthening as economies around the world invest heavily in expanding their weapons production capacity in the face of growing global conflicts and challenges.

Based on the previous agreement on the further development of Iran's nuclear energy program, the following states parties to this agreement, the USA, Great Britain, the EU (Germany and France), but also China and Russia, are among the largest weapons producers in the world.

According to SIPRI reported by Trading Economics, military spending in 2024 reached an all-time high of US$997,309 million in the US, an all-time high of US$88,458.50 million in Germany, an all-time high of US$64,675.00 million in France, an all-time high of US$81,763.20 million in the UK, an all-time high of US$313,658.30 million in China and an all-time high of US$148,967.30 million in Russia.

Antimony prices are forecast to keep rising due to a gain in the momentum of the demand as a result of not only massive investments in defense production, but also to the use of this raw material in the green energy and electronics industries. The most popular applications of this raw material are flame retardants, batteries, and semiconductors. As Chinese exports of antimony are subject to increased scrutiny, particularly in the wake of a global trade conflict, leading to supply chain disruptions, among other things, supply becomes insufficient to meet demand. This is leading to price pressure. The antimony price—estimated at $20,000 per ton in Mandalay (production and cost forecast for the full year 2025)—has already scaled up two and a half times this year, and speculators see no end to this growth trend in the antimony price per ton. In fact, CarbonCredits.Com writes:

The surge comes from a supply shortage in China, Russia, and Southeast Asia. At the same time, demand is rising in the solar sector, fire retardants, and military uses.

Recent posts on X show that the shortage is still affecting availability. Prices have reached $51,500 per ton in 2025. Some market speculation suggests that prices could reach $100,000 per ton.

The website carboncredits-dot.com reports the following chart on the "Antimony Price" of the S&P Global

Improved Profitability

Assuming the bullish gold price forecast is correct, and the yellow metal sells for $4,000 or even $5,000 per ounce in the coming quarters, Mandalay's profit margin, which starts to build on an AISC of $1,795 to $1,975 per ounce, is one thing. The profit margin achieved by the combined company, with a remarkably lower AISC of $1,760 per ounce in 2025 or even $1,420 per ounce in 2026, is another matter entirely. The impact on the new company's stock price promises to be incredibly positive, as rising profitability is a key factor in the sentiment of North American equity markets.

For the first three months of 2025, Mandalay Resources shareholders benefited from a 65% year-on-year jump in adjusted EBITDA to US$44.1 million and a 151% year-on-year jump in consolidated net income to US$14.8 million, supported by consolidated revenue of US$78.1 million, a 41% year-on-year increase.

Mandalay Resources achieved an astonishing boost in profitability by producing 20,376 ounces of gold and 161 tonnes of antimony in Q1-2025. This was supported by a 35.3% year-over-year increase in the gold margin (average realised gold price/oz less All-in sustaining costs/oz gold eq. produced) to US$1,042 in Q1-2025. It's easy to imagine how the combined company's profitability could evolve if 180,000 ounces are produced in 2026, supported by a margin of US$3,580/oz gold eq. This margin is absolutely possible if veteran investors and billionaires consider a spot price of US$5,000 for gold to be within reach.

Not to mention the impact of the electronics hype and the arms race-driven antimony on future business: Mandalay Resources produced 161 tonnes and sold 196 tonnes in the first quarter of 2025 at an average antimony price of $34,923 per tonne (up 152.64% year-on-year). It's easy to imagine the impact that the Costerfield mine's projected annual antimony production of around 2,800 tonnes will have on the combined company's profitability.

Seeking Alpha reports that we have already:

seen prices surge 250% in 2024 following China's ban and an ongoing global supply shortage.

Recently, the price of antimony has already reached $50,000 per ton, and speculators expect it to reach around $100,000 per ton, according to the aforementioned website CarbonCredits.Com.

Mandalay-Alkane Merger: Greater Visibility for Shares on More Markets

The aforementioned Globe Newswire adds:

Following completion of the Transaction, the combined company will continue operating as "Alkane Resources", remain listed on the ASX and is seeking a listing on the TSX exchange.

The enhanced popularity due to the potential listing on more exchanges will benefit Mandalay's current shareholders. Should they currently complain about the lack of popularity due to limited daily trading volume, their shares will likely have a greater market presence after the merger and will be brought to the attention of a larger and more diverse community of market participants. In fact, the aforementioned Globe Newswire also writes:

Improved capital market positioning anticipated to drive valuation re-rate. The combined company is expected to benefit from a primary ASX listing valuation re-rate driven by GDXJ and potential ASX 300 index inclusion. Further value should be unlocked through greater trading liquidity, a larger free-float and a more diverse shareholder base as the combined company continues to grow.

Mandalay Resources shares are currently affected by low trading volumes, but in our view, with higher daily trading volumes, the combined company's shares will be more sensitive to market sentiment, affecting prices, and ultimately, this will also serve as an incentive to mobilise more analysts to cover the stock. Ratings will follow each other much more frequently, and not sporadically as now, and report on the characteristics of a portfolio of metals activity that is consistent with the future economic project. The metals that the merged company will produce, including the projects to develop and install copper production, will be in high demand as we seek to grow through greater environmental awareness, sustainability, and safety, and to be protected from disruptions or potential negative external influences.

The Financial Condition of the Combined Business

The aforementioned Globe Newswire adds:

Creates a powerful platform with a shared vision for continued growth. Robust balance sheet with a proforma cash balance of A$188 / C$167 million 3 at 31 March 2025 to pursue organic and inorganic growth.

With a larger size of business and greater visibility in the financial markets, particularly among analysts, the combined company will also have easier access to debt capital than Alkane, potentially by expanding its existing banking relationships. Mandalay Resources Corporation maintains a debt-free balance sheet, while Alkane Resources has total debt of $38.8 million. This resulted in interest expenses of $1 million over the last 12 months, more than offset by operating income of $14.9 million over the last 12 months.

The start of a new economic cycle is leading to a cooling of borrowing costs, as both the Bank of Canada and the Reserve Bank of Australia ("RBA") are cutting interest rates. This will make it easier to raise the necessary capital to expand gold production and support future developments in copper production.

The aforementioned Globe Newswire adds:

The combined company plans to continue to invest in exploration at all three of its producing mines and pursue other growth opportunities in addition to the Boda-Kaiser copper-gold project which adds future production potential with its significant scale.

A scoping study for Alkane Resources' Boda-Kaiser copper-gold project in New South Wales examined three possible scenarios, including mining and processing 20 million, 10 million, and 5 million tonnes of ore per year. The first scenario is likely to be chosen due to the metals' far more favourable price environment than previously thought and the potential to deliver significantly greater value; it would be a real shame not to capitalise on the first scenario. In the largest production scenario, the Boda-Kaiser copper-gold complex produces an average of 225,350 gold equivalent ounces annually over a 17-year mine life at an AISC of A$1,901.8/oz Au Eq. (or approximately US$1,242.44/ounce at the time of writing). This would make the Boda-Kaiser copper/gold complex a serious candidate for low-cost production, as AISCs are close to US$ 1,500/ounce worldwide, according to the World Gold Council chart.

The estimated pre-tax internal rate of return (IRR) of 24% was calculated based on a long-term gold price of A$3,500 per ounce and a copper price of A$15,000 per tonne (or approximately US$2,279 per ounce of gold and US$9,767.13 per tonne of copper at the time of writing). At a current gold price of US$3,361.09 per troy ounce and a copper futures price (HG1:COM) of US$4.82 per pound, or approximately US$10,626 per tonne, the IRR is significantly higher, between 30 and 35%. However, even at 24%, the Boda-Kaiser copper/gold complex is considered a financially sound project, as we have previously seen mineral metals projects with IRRs in the 15 to 25 percent range.

Not to mention future prices: The price of gold is heading towards US$ 4,000/ounce or even US$ 5,000/ounce, and the price of copper is on the trajectory to spike 4 times from current levels in the next few years, according to hedge fund manager Pierre Andurand's opinion last year. Copper is a key metal for electrification programs, the rollout of electric vehicles, the development of technologies for harnessing renewable energy sources, and the expansion of data centres with AI software. Given this strong demand, supply will struggle to keep pace.

The Boda Kaiser copper-gold project in New South Wales requires an investment of A$ 1.783 million (approximately US$1,160.99 million at the time of writing). We expect it will take some time to secure the necessary cash collateral to successfully obtain the required borrowings. The most realistic scenario is a phased approach to the Boda Kaiser copper-gold project. We draw this conclusion based on our previous analysis of Harmony Gold Mining Company Limited's (HMY) US$1.03 billion deal to fully acquire MAC Copper Limited (MTAL). It is true that MAC Copper's only asset at the CSA copper mine in New South Wales, Australia, is merely copper production of 41,000 tonnes by 2024, or potentially >50,000 tonnes per year by 2026, with a reserve life of more than 12 years, while Boda-Kaiser Copper-Gold also consists of surging gold bullion. However, while the bridge financing of up to US$1.25 billion that HMY can obtain from branches of Citibank, JPMorgan Chase Bank and Macquarie Bank Limited to finance the acquisition is secured by HMY's cash balance of US$1.14 billion, Mandalay and Alkane instead have combined cash and ST investments of approximately US$110 million as of March 31, 2025.

But the nature of the merger also makes it clear that the companies will need time to raise the cash required, and on which advance the financing applications, as it is evident that capital is lacking at present, or that project development will be gradual, proceeding step by step as funds are available. The merger will be carried out through an all-stock transaction. While such transactions are generally frowned upon by investors due to the risk of share dilution, in the case of the merger of Mandalay Resources with Alkane Resources, the all-stock transaction helps to resolve issues. It increases the daily trading volume, and if the new entity that will emerge from the merger decides to also list on the Toronto market, well, without taking anything from the Australian market, the Canadian market is already much more popular and trafficked than the Australian one, so that also makes trading much more attractive and interesting in terms of opportunities and their breadth.

Stock Prices: Bright Future Outlook From the Merger, but Volatility Offers a Possible Dip

At the time of writing, Mandalay Resources Corporation shares were trading under the symbol MNDJF on the U.S. OTCQB exchange at a price of $3.81 per share, representing a market capitalisation of $355.76 million.

Shares are trading very high in the cycle, trading very close to the upper end of the 52-week range of $1.45 to $4.14 per share. They are also above the MA Ribbon, and the RSI of 55.17 suggests that investors looking to strengthen their positions in order to have a larger stake in the merged company have ample downside room to bid significantly lower share prices.

Fed officials are concerned about the high level of uncertainty following more than two months of global trade disputes, as noted at the May FOMC meeting, with the risk of rising inflation remaining elevated for the upcoming months. The Fed is therefore being patient and waiting for more clarity on inflation and economic developments. The Fed maintained its target range for the federal funds rate at 4.25-4.50% for the third consecutive meeting in May 2025, but given the "wait and see" approach, headwinds for gold prices, a zero-yielding asset, will come from a higher-for-longer interest rates environment. This would pave the way for a decline in Mandalay Resources shares, consistent with the positive correlation between gold prices and Mandalay Resources' share price.

Conclusion

Mandalay Resources Corporation, a Canadian gold equivalent producer with facilities in Australia and Sweden, will merge with Australian miner Alkane Resources, which operates a gold mine in Australia and a copper project. Investing in Mandalay today offers the opportunity to acquire shares in a larger, more diversified, and value-creating company with growth potential and a more ample market presence. Potential investors may want to seek a significantly lower price than currently available.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: 2 Dividend Stocks I'd Marry If I Could
data.description:
Introduction

I think we can all agree that this year has been quite something.

  • We have been through one of the fastest bear markets ever.
  • This is one of the most headline-driven markets due to the tariff war.
  • On a short-term basis, we are somewhere between tariff-driven inflation and demand-weakness-related disinflation, which makes short-term macro predictions quite useless.
  • The dollar is under tremendous pressure, as bond yields are rising, further pressuring the government's plans to finance close to $7 trillion in debt this year. It also doesn't help that the U.S. is expected to maintain a very high deficit, with almost no chance of cutting spending without hurting growth (I think).

  • Related to the point above, international stocks have outperformed the S&P 500 quite substantially, as the iShares MSCI ACWI ex U.S. ETF (ACWX) is up 16% year-to-date, 'crushing' the S&P 500 by a wide margin.

  • Cyclical economic growth remains in a tough spot, as the main drivers of growth remain secular trends like the data center buildout, economic re-shoring, and programs related to government spending.

Needless to say, these are just a few points I came up with, as the bigger picture is way more complex than this. And to make matters worse, almost all of these factors are in some way related.

That's also why Liz Ann Sonders and Kevin Gordon of Charles Schwab presented such a diverse view in their 2025 Mid-Year Outlook, writing that:

One of the more difficult aspects of analyzing the market this year has been the significant shifts in sector leadership. Shown below is the fan favorite sector quilt; this version breaks performance down into rolling two-week periods. As you can see, there isn't one sector that has maintained the top position for more than two weeks. Perhaps more interesting is the fact that even though Energy was in first place for three of the two-week periods (the most of any sector), it is one of the worst-performing sectors year-to-date. Conversely, Industrials only held the top spot once, yet is leading year-to-date. - Charles Schwab

That's also why Schwab makes that case that "a ton of sector churn" will be driven by issues like tariff policymaking, which will make it incredibly hard to make sector recommendations, especially because of the ripple effect, which requires next-level thinking, as the winners and losers of the trade war may not even be that obvious on first sight.

We continue to expect a ton of sector churn, driven by the mercurial nature of tariff policymaking, which in turn will continue to make it difficult to make sector recommendations (we remain neutral on all 11 sectors). In fairness, there are sectors and industries that are at or near the epicenter of aggressive tariffs—such as import-dependent consumer businesses, for example. Yet, there are ripple effects associated with the potential for weaker spending and investment, which would ultimately hit a sector like Financials (which is looked at as a haven from the trade war these days). - Charles Schwab

To put it differently, stock picking has become a fascinating endeavour.

On the one hand, it is nearly impossible to pick stocks based on a short-term and mid-term thesis.

On the other hand, because of elevated volatility and unusual market behavior, we can buy fantastic companies if we apply the right long-term mindset. Stocks that may even benefit from volatility and/or offer attractive long-term value through attractive valuations and wide-moat business models.

Hence, based on everything we discussed so far, I'm using the second part of this article to present two of my all-time favorite dividend (growth) stocks that I would even own if I could own just 5 individual dividend stocks.

They perfectly fit my strategy, have wide-moat business models, the ability to beat the market, and everything it takes to consistently hike dividends.

CME Group (CME) - It's Hard To Come Up With A Better Idea In This Market

I have brought up CME Group on a semi-frequent basis over the past few years, and I'm not getting tired of it.

For starters, CME Group has the perfect business model for this market, as it is the world's largest operator of futures exchanges. Initially, it was formed through strategic mergers, including the Chicago Mercantile Exchange, the Chicago Board of Trade ("CBOT"), the New York Mercantile Exchange ("NYMEX"), and others.

As a result, almost all of its revenues come from transaction fees, with additional revenues from clearing and data services. It dominates areas like interest rates, agricultural commodities, energy, metals, and cryptocurrencies.

In addition to long-term secular growth from the ever-increasing demand for hedging products from corporations and increasing trading volumes, the company's business model allows it to benefit from elevated volatility, unlike almost any other company.

On top of benefiting from elevated volatility from the uncertainty I mentioned in the introduction, the company has grown its average daily volumes ("ADV") by 7% per year since 2015, boosting its net income by 12% per year.

Moreover, since 2020, it has grown its international ADVs by 10% per year, as it has shifted its focus to booming growth in international markets. Currently, more than 60% of its front office sales staff work outside the U.S.

On top of that, CME benefits from the fact that scaling its operations is super-efficient, as almost all trading is done online. Hence, products launched over the past five years created 2.4 million ADVs in 2024, boosted by micros (smaller futures), options, fixed income, energy commodities, and alternatives like crypto.

Furthermore, growth from elevated global volatility is expected to get support from data developments, as AI and other technologies allow retail investors to access data that used to be reserved for institutional clients only. CME expects this to support long-term growth. I agree with that, as AI is bullish for trading volumes instead of a disruptor of CME's business.

It has also shrugged off any concerns regarding new competition. While two years ago, the company started getting downgrades due to the new entrance of FMX, it has proven that its size gives it a clear edge in pricing and liquidity.

And, as a cherry on top, the company also has one of the S&P 500's best margin profiles, as it turned $6.1 billion in revenues last year into $3.7 billion of net income, a net margin of slightly more than 60%.

With regard to its dividend, the company, which has a rare AA- credit rating, usually distributes 100% of its free cash flow to shareholders. It does this through four regular quarterly dividends and one special dividend.

The quarterly dividend yields 1.8%. I expect its annualized dividend yield to be somewhere in the 4.0% to 4.5% range, as analysts expect free cash flow to rise to more than $4.4 billion by 2027. I am more bullish than that, purely based on my "higher for longer" thesis, which includes volatility.

That's also why I expect CME shares to return more than 13% per year going forward, as it trades at 25.3x earnings. While that may seem lofty, it's below its multi-decade average of 27.1x, and boosted by its impressive margin profile and mid-to-high single-digit annual EPS growth.

Because its margins are so high, and it returns almost everything to shareholders, investors apply a higher multiple, as earnings are "high quality."

In other words, to me, CME is the perfect stock for this market environment and one of my all-time favorite dividend stocks, in general.

Union Pacific Corp. (UNP) - A Cyclical Wide-Moat Gem Poised To Outperform

Union Pacific was one of the first five stocks I bought for my dividend portfolio. It's also a company I cannot imagine selling, ever.

While Union Pacific is a cyclical company, it has withstood the test of time, as it is one of just two Class I railroads that dominates the Western two-thirds of the United States, which includes servicing a network of 32,000 railroad miles across 23 states, connecting major ports and supporting almost every single supply chain you can think of.

On top of having limited competition, the U.S. would literally run into massive trouble if Union Pacific were to cease all operations overnight. While that will never happen, I like to use this as an example of how important Union Pacific is.

Moreover, under Jim Vena, who became CEO in 2023, the company has become the most efficient Class I railroad in all of North America, as it has improved its operations, boosted customer satisfaction, and lowered costs without sacrificing its ability to grow volumes.

While I may sound a bit dramatic, I believe Jim Vena and his team have shown the world a masterclass in railroading, with expectations of outperforming revenues, strong pricing dollars (above inflation), and further efficiency gains through at least 2027.

Moreover, as of June 10, when the company presented at the Wells Fargo Industrials & Materials Conference, it has seen an upswing in volumes, despite cyclical challenges.

Quarter-to-date, carloads are up 5%, driven by double-digit growth in bulk, a segment I am very bullish on, as it comes with strong secular growth in agriculture, chemicals, and support from intermodal due to economic re-shoring and truck-to-rail conversions.

On top of that, I am bullish on cyclical growth despite the elevated uncertainty we are dealing with right now. I elaborated on that in a recent article titled "Buried Under Fear: One Of The Best Opportunities I've Seen In Years."

It also needs to be said that despite cyclical headwinds, UNP always bounces back, as no recession in history has done lasting damage to the company.

In fact, the company, which has an A- credit rating, has a weighted average rate on its debt of just 3.8%, and a weighted average maturity of roughly 20 years, which is better than almost any company on my radar.

Thanks to this balance sheet, investors have consistently benefited from its success, as the company has hiked its dividend for 18 consecutive years, with a CAGR of 17%. During this period, it has also bought back 44% of its shares and boosted its return on invested capital to roughly 16%, an indication of excellence when it comes to investing in its own business.

Currently, UNP yields 2.4%, with a 47% payout ratio and a five-year CAGR of 6.7%.

I expect this number to accelerate, as analysts seem to agree with my thesis of stronger earnings growth.

Using the FactSet data in the chart below, analysts expect 4% EPS growth this year, potentially followed by 11% and 10% growth in 2026 and 2027, respectively.

If we get a bottom in leading indicators like the ISM Manufacturing Index, which marked the peak of UNP in 2021, we could see an annual return of at least 11-12% based on its 19.8x P/E ratio. I highlighted the 2021 peak of the ISM Manufacturing Index in the chart below.

For now, the market isn't expecting a growth acceleration, which is why EPS growth numbers mainly rely on the company's investments, buybacks, and secular tailwind.

In other words, while UNP is very different from CME, it remains one of my all-time favorite dividend stocks and a great company to buy when sentiment toward cyclical stocks is poor.

That's also why I expect the ratio of Union Pacific to the S&P 500 to bottom this year, which could lead to a return to the 2000-2021 period when Union Pacific consistently outperformed the market.

In the weeks ahead, I'll shed more light on high-quality cyclical dividend stocks, as I believe there are a lot of overlooked quality stocks that make tremendous investments. This includes lower-yielding growers and higher-yielding income stocks.

For now, this is my takeaway:

Takeaway

This market is a puzzle. It's volatile, headline-driven, and full of contradictions, which makes it one of the toughest markets in many years.

As a result, short-term predictions are almost useless, which is just another reason why I feel my long-term dividend strategy is the best way to go.

In this article, I went a step further, as I discussed two of my all-time favorite dividend stocks, which I expect to deliver tremendous long-term value for a wide range of dividend investors.

  • CME thrives on uncertainty. As it's the world's largest futures exchange, it profits from volatility, while it also maintains super-high margins and a cash-rich business model. It also stands out because of its special dividend policy.

  • UNP is a cyclical powerhouse with a wide moat. Under Jim Vena's leadership, it has become the most efficient railroad in North America. Despite near-term economic growth fears, its volume rebound, pricing power, and 18-year dividend growth streak make it a cornerstone holding in all of my family's dividend portfolios.

Needless to say, all of this explains why we have consistently bought more of these stocks, using stock price weakness to our advantage.

Given my high conviction, that's the playbook I will continue to implement.

Risks To My Thesis

Besides general market and recession risks, I believe general risks to my thesis are limited, as CME's biggest risk is a prolonged period of subdued volatility and new competition.

Union Pacific's biggest risk is operational accidents, a failure to maintain high on-time rates, and the inability to benefit from growth drivers like economic re-shoring. Temporary risks are economic weakness and a loose trucking market, which could pressure intermodal transportation volumes and pricing.

data.title: Eurozone Industrial Production Unexpectedly Nosedives In April
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski

Eurozone industrial production dropped sharply in April, partly wiping out the front-loading surge of the first quarter.

After an impressive first quarter, eurozone industrial production staged an expected nosedive in April, dropping by 2.4% month-on-month. On the year, it is still up by 0.8%. After the strong surge in the first quarter on the back of US front-loading of eurozone goods ahead of higher tariffs, industrial production showed the expected reversal. However, looking at the individual countries shows large divergence across the eurozone and once again a sharp distortion of eurozone data due to Ireland (which had a month-on-month drop by more than 15%). While Germany, France and Spain saw industrial production dropping, Italy recorded some improvement.

Industrial outlook between tariffs, rising oil prices and a turning inventory cycle

The eurozone manufacturing sector is currently highly affected by two main factors: the cyclical turning of the inventory cycle and Donald Trump’s tariffs. Tariffs and the related front-loading had been the main driver of surging industrial production in the first quarter. Lower energy prices as a result of the tariff announcements and erratic economic policy announcements in the US also seemed to improve the outlook for industry. It would seem that Israel’s military strikes against Iran last night have clearly erased hopes for sustainably low oil prices. With Brent trading again close to 80 USD/b, energy prices are back on the eurozone manufacturers’ worry list.

US tariffs and again highly elevated geopolitical risks provide reasons enough against any premature optimism. However, more fundamentally speaking, it looks like eurozone manufacturing has been bottoming out. The announced German fiscal stimulus, as well as European defense spending, have both added to order books and general optimism. But there's more. After huge stockpiling in 2021 and 2022 and high inventories limiting production since then, the inventory cycle is showing the first signs of turning. Even though it's not happening yet in all eurozone countries, it is most pronounced in Germany. Still, it is the combination of inventory reduction and improving order books that will boost production in the future.

So, while there are signs of optimism, the outlook for the eurozone's manufacturing sector remains incredibly uncertain. Just when things were starting to look more positive, geopolitical risks and those rising oil prices are once again casting a long shadow over recovery hopes.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Israel Strikes Iran's Nuclear And Military Sites
data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Threat of war

Tensions are intensifying in the Middle East as Israel launched "pre-emptive" strikes on Iran's nuclear facilities and military sites, following which it declared a state of emergency in anticipation of retaliation from Tehran. Iran responded by launching over 100 drones at Israel overnight, heightening the risk of an all-out war in the region.

Conflict escalates: Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu announced the launch of "Operation Rising Lion," which he said was a targeted military operation to "roll back the Iranian threat to Israel's very survival." Iran's supreme leader Ayatollah Khamenei warned of retaliation, saying Israel "has prepared for itself a bitter, painful fate." The International Atomic Energy Agency confirmed that Iran's Natanz nuclear facility was hit, but no increase in radiation levels was observed. As for Iran's drones, Israeli military spokesman Effie Deffrin said defense systems would intercept the threats.

U.S. stance: Secretary of State Marco Rubio said the U.S. was not involved in Israel's attack on Iran, adding that Israel informed Washington that the strikes were being carried out for self-defense. Prior to the attack, President Donald Trump had warned of a potential strike. To note, the U.S. had already pulled personnel from the Middle East earlier this week due to heightened regional tensions.

Markets react: While the world suspected Israel would launch an attack on Iran, the severity took markets by surprise, with oil prices surging 13% and U.S. stock futures down more than 1%. The U.S. and Iran were slated to continue nuclear talks this weekend, but now it's unclear if they'll go ahead. ING Economic and Financial Analysis flagged the risk of disruptions to Iranian oil supplies as well as shipping through the Strait of Hormuz, which would significantly impact the global LNG market.

Here's the latest Seeking Alpha analysis

Nvidia: On The Road To $4T Valuation

Buy Intel Low Before It Explodes Higher

Dividend Investors: Understand Your Risks

Rapidly Growing 7%+ Yield: My Favorite High-Yield Fund Today

Compound Your Retirement With These 5 Monthly Dividend REITs

What else is happening...

Boeing (BA) 787 crash: Scrutiny on airplane configuration at takeoff.

Trump calls Musk 'a friend,' blasts Fed's Powell for not cutting rates.

CBO estimates Trump's 'One Big, Beautiful Bill' to hurt the poorest.

Sens. Sanders, King introduce bill to end prescription drug ads.

Thermo Fisher (TMO) looks to sell part of its diagnostics business.

China delays approval for Synopsys (SNPS)-Ansys (ANSS) deal.

META ropes in AI firm Scale's CEO to head superintelligence team.

Tech firms' new automation AI tools pose threat to ad agencies.

Chime Financial (CHYM) shares jump 37% in first day of trading.

Coinbase (COIN) CEO: Bitcoin is new 'refuge' as U.S. debt swells.

Today's Markets

In Asia, Japan -0.9%. Hong Kong -0.6%. China -0.8%. India -0.7%.
In Europe, at midday, London -0.5%. Paris -1.2%. Frankfurt -1.6%.
Futures at 6:30, Dow -1.1%. S&P -1.1%. Nasdaq -1.4%. Crude +9% to $74.14. Gold +1.2% to $3,442.70. Bitcoin -2.3% to $104,838.
Ten-year Treasury Yield +1 bp to 4.38%.

On The Calendar

No major earnings scheduled for today.

See today's economic calendar on Seeking Alpha.

data.title: Israel's Strike Lifts Dollar But Only Modestly, Gold And Oil Rally
data.description:
Overview

Israel attacked Iranian nuclear enrichment site and apparently targeted scientists and top Revolutionary Guard leadership. Reports suggest that no increase in radioactivity has been detected. The US quickly indicated that while it was informed of the attack, it did not authorize it. President Trump did warn of a possible strike, but the press reports made it seem as if a strike was not imminent. The dollar is stronger against all the G10 currencies and most emerging market currencies; However, the gains have been limited, and some will question the dollar's safe haven status. Equities are heavier, with most European bourses off 1.0-1.5%, while US index futures are off the equivalent. Asia Pacific bourses were off by less.

Bond markets have not benefitted. European 10-year benchmark yields are mostly 2-4 bps higher. The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is off a single basis point to near 4.35%. Gold, which the ECB estimated earlier this week to have replaced the euro as the second most important reserve asset after the dollar, is up a little more than 1% in late European morning turnover near $3386. July WTI is up about 7.5% near $69.

USD: Israel's strike on Iran has seen the Dollar Index recoup yesterday's losses that had seen it fall to a new three-year low in early North American turnover yesterday near 97.60. However, it has not been able to take out yesterday's high near 98.50, which will lead to talk about it losing its safe haven status, but strong conclusions will require some time. For release today, only the preliminary University of Michigan's consumer sentiment is due. A small improvement in confidence and a slight decline in inflation expectations are expected. In the tightening cycle, Fed Chair Powell pointed to the University of Michigan survey and sometimes, even the preliminary report; now, less so. The NY Fed's survey has not been as alarmist. Another way to get a handle on inflation expectations is derived from the market, such as the difference between yield on conventional instruments and the inflation-protected securities. The one-year breakeven, for example, is near 2.50%, the low for the year. The 5-10 year breakevens are a couple of basis points, around 2.30%.

Euro: The euro reached roughly $1.1630 in early North American dealing yesterday. It has not been this high since October 2021. It settled above it its upper Bollinger Band yesterday but was sold to about $1.1510 on Israel's strike and has held above yesterday's low near $1.1485. The eurozone industrial output fell sharply in April (-2.4%) and the trade surplus was halved to 14 bln euros (from a record surplus of nearly 29 bln euros in March). This is broadly consistent with the return to a slower growth trajectory after the 0.6% quarter-over-quarter expansion in Q1 25, which matched the strongest growth since Q2 22. However, the median forecast in Bloomberg's survey anticipates growth slowing to 0.1% in Q2 25 and Q3 25. The swaps market has slightly more than a 10% chance of a rate cut next month, but it rises to almost 60% for the September meeting, 75% for the October meeting, and it is nearly completely discounted before the end of the year.

CNY: The broadly weaker US dollar was threatening to end the yuan's consolidative phase. The greenback was turned down on Wednesday after briefly trading north of CNH7.20. It fell back toward CNH7.1715 yesterday. The broad but modest dollar gains lifted the greenback to almost CNH7.19 today. The PBOC set the dollar's reference rate lower for the fourth consecutive session (CNY7.1772) and below CNY7.18 for the first time since April 1. China reported May lending figures that were in line with expectations. Early Monday, it will release May real sector data. The economy appears to continue to struggle to extend momentum.

JPY: The dollar was turned back on Wednesday after setting a new high for the month near JPY145.45. It reached almost JPY143.20 yesterday. The dollar initially fell to around JPY142.80 a new six-day low today on Israel's strike but recovered to almost JPY144 in the European morning. After becoming decoupled from US 10-year yield, the traditional driver, the rolling 30-day correlation is rising, and slightly above 0.40, it is at the highest in about two months. Last week, Japan's 0.7% annualized contraction in Q1 was revised to a more modest -0.2%. Still, Q2 has begun off poorly. Earlier today, Japan revised down April's 0.9% contraction in industrial output to -1.1% and the tertiary industry index (services) may eke out a modest 0.3% gain (after March's 0.3% decline was revised to -1.0%). The BOJ meets next week, but despite the firm inflation readings, seems to be in no hurry to raise rates. Indeed, the focus may not be on interest rate policy but on the BOJ's bond purchases. The BOJ has slowed its bond purchases, but this may have contributed to the volatility of the long end of the curve. It will reportedly consider making smaller reductions from the current pace of JPY400 bln (~$2.8 bln) a quarter. Some think the pace could be halved to JPY200 bln. At the end of March, the BOJ's balance sheet was about 118% of GDP, the smallest since May 2020. It peaked in 2022 near 133% of GDP.

GBP: Sterling set a new three-year high yesterday near $1.3625. The gains were despite weak jobs data and an unexpectedly large contraction in April's GDP, which boosted confidence of a rate cut in August (not next week). It made a marginal new high earlier today, slightly above $1.3630 before selling off to take out yesterday's low by about 1/100 of a cent (according to Bloomberg pricing). Sterling's weakness, however, is evident against the euro, where it has fallen to new lows since early May. The euro reached almost GBP0.8550, which corresponds to the (50%) retracement of the euro's losses since the April 11 high near GBP0.8740. It is consolidating today.

CAD: After reversing lower on Tuesday from around CAD1.3730, the greenback fell to CAD1.3600 yesterday, its lowest level since last October and dipped briefly below it in early trading before Israel's strike. It recovered to about CAD1.3650 before consolidating. Canada reports manufacturing and wholesale sales and capacity utilization figures. These do not capture the attention of the market, even in the best of times. Next week's highlights include April portfolio flows and retail sales. The swaps market has a little more than one cut discounted by the end of the year, which is currently seen as the end of the easing cycle.

AUD: The Australian dollar recovered from an eight-day low in early European turnover yesterday (almost $0.6475) to reach the $0.6535 area in the North American afternoon. Israel's military operation drove it to nearly $0.6455. It has subsequently recovered almost $0.6500 and is consolidating in the European morning above around $0.6480.

MXN: After falling to its lowest level since last August on Wednesday (~MXN18.9120), the greenback consolidated yesterday between approximately MXN18.8560 and MXN18.98. The peso is one of the only emerging market currencies that trades 24-hours a day. It is sometimes, therefore, used as a proxy for other emerging market currencies, especially in a risk-off event. The dollar spiked to around MXN19.08, but is hovering now near MXN19.00. In the recent past, such spikes were not sustained. Meanwhile, the above 4% inflation reported for May appears to have spurred some disagreement about the central bank's leadership. Deputy Governor Heath argued for a pause, while Governor Rodriguez continued to advocate for a 50 bps cut. The central bank meets on June 26. A compromise might be a 25 bp cut, and that is what the swaps market appears to be discounting as the most likely scenario.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Papa John's: Valuation Agrees With The Premium Price Of Acquisition
data.description:
A few months after my previous coverage, Papa John’s International, Inc. (NASDAQ:PZZA) is making a comeback amid its strong rally for four weeks straight. Finally, it’s moving toward my target price. There are various factors driving this uptrend. But in the past two days, it has made a bigger noise after the news about the joint acquisition proposal by Apollo Global Management, Inc. (APO) and Irth Capital Management came out. I have some possible scenarios in mind, which I intend to discuss later. But fundamentally, the increase is justifiable. After all, I explained in my previous article that the correction was already too much. I will try to discuss all aspects and give my recommendations.

PZZA Q1 2025: A Brief Review

In my previous article, the lingering impact of inflationary headwinds on Papa John’s weakened its performance. Inflexible pricing, narrowing the pricing gap between QSRs and fast-casual diners, and softer consumer spending were the notable causes. Yet, there have been some improvements from my previous coverage.

At the beginning of the year, Q1 2025 revenues amounted to $518.3M. It improved by 0.9% YoY from $513.9M. This was also the first time its revenue had a YoY increase since 2023, showing its recovery. Its international markets remained the cornerstone of its recovery. As we can see, its comparable sales growth YoY was 3.2%, while SYS growth reached 5.7%. These completely offset PZZA’s weakness in North America. This should not be surprising considering its faster expansion in international markets.

With respect to its profitability, PZZA became less cost-sensitive as the operating leverage dropped from 71.6% to 70.6%. The increase in costs and expenses was mainly due to fixed costs. So, its operating margin decreased from 6.6% to 4.7%. Nonetheless, the idea that PZZA remained profitable shows it still generated enough revenue to cover its costs and expenses amid its expansion.

Acquisition And Possible Scenarios

The past four weeks have been very sweet for PZZA investors. We can point to its post-correction rebound. But what has been strengthening the uptrend is the joint proposal of APO and Irth to acquire PZZA for $2B and take it private for about $60 per share. Following the news, the stock rose by 7.5% in a day, the highest in seven months. Considering this, there are some scenarios ahead of the stakeholders.

First, PZZA will accept the proposal. Considering the current price range of $50-51, investors may realize a 15-25% gain. I assumed the offer price at $60-64.

Second, the proposal gets rejected instantly. We may expect the stock to weaken amid uncertainty and/or pessimism among investors. The price may return to $45-46. That was where the stock was trading before the news came out. If I include a 5% risk allowance, the stock will reach $43. This makes the stock even cheaper. I should disagree with a further dip. As I’ve said before, the correction was a bit too stretched for its valuation.

Third, PZZA asks for a higher amount considering its valuation. If we look at its P/E multiple (Scenario 3a), PZZA trades at 21x its earnings. This is lower than the three-year average of 28x. So, there is a possibility for it to ask for a higher buyout price. If the companies agree with the 28x multiple, PZZA will increase to $67. Otherwise, they can compromise at a lower valuation say 26x. This will give a value of $62. If it bases its decision on P/S (Scenario 3b) we can expect the price range of 64-76. I will discuss this further later. This is interesting to look at. It shows that the current bid price is still justifiable for its valuation. Hence, PZZA is still cheap, and entering at the current price is justifiable.

I Am On The Buy Side Regardless Of The Deal Outcome

Amid its weaker performance in North America, investors may see the acquisition as PZZA’s end game. But we also wonder what will happen to PZZA should it reject the proposal. Sustainability is one of the key concerns.

If we assess the company based on its most recent performance, I can say that it remains sustainable. One aspect to consider is its viability and positive cash flow despite market headwinds. The balance sheet improved from my previous coverage as the cash level increased by $6M. This shows that the company still generates returns despite its increased number of stores in a volatile environment. It has enough earnings to cover its operating capacity. As we can see, its cash level has rebounded in the past five quarters. Its $19M FCF last Q1 shows that it converted about 4% of its revenues into free cash after capex.

Moreover, its borrowings remain manageable. Its Net Debt/EBITDA also improved a lot. In my previous article, it was 3.x. Now, it's only 2.8x. It will only take about three years to cover all borrowings. PZZA can cover it since its borrowings have longer maturities. Its senior notes of $400M will mature in September 2029. Meanwhile, $145M of $200M term loans have maturities of at least five years. Considering this, PZZA has enough time and capital buffer for borrowing repayments.

Aside from its decent earnings and balance sheet, we have to note that the majority of its stores are franchised. They comprise 84% of the total restaurants in North America and 99% in international markets. So, its capital intensity is manageable. I know I already explained this before, but I would like to highlight this again since franchising than owning stores is less capital intensive and less cost-sensitive.

Valuation

In relation to the third scenario I explained earlier, I believe that PZZA is still trading at a discount despite its post-correction recovery. From April 15 to June 12, the stock has already increased by 67%. In my opinion, that was warranted considering its valuation.

I already explained that its P/E multiple appears much cheaper than in previous years. The TP of $67.37 will give a 33% upside potential. Its sales per share or SPS shows the same thing. The stock only trades at 0.80x. This is much cheaper than the average of 1.20x. If we use this to get its fair value, the TP will be $75.73, a 50% upside potential. PZZA can use this multiple to increase the offer price. If APO and Irth refuses, they can still meet halfway at 1.00x. This will give a TP of $64.02 and a 24% upside potential. Considering this, both price ratios show that APO's offer is realistic.

Technicals

PZZA has already had bullish signals even before the deal. The price crossed above the 50-day SMA and the 200-day SMA last May 30 as buying volume increased. In addition, both SMAs have already flattened with tendencies to rebound as they convey a positive shift in market sentiments. This could be insider buying at some level. But the stock market as a whole is regaining its momentum after the correction.

MACD and RSI mirror the price trend, so we cannot detect any bearish divergence. The possible cause of its weakness yesterday was overbuying. RSI has already exceeded 70, which prompted profit-taking at $50-52. This may lead to a dip if selling volume exceeds buying. From the looks of it, buyers are waiting for a better entry point. This appears consistent with the Fibonacci Retracement since buying volume weakens as it approaches the 0.786 retracement point. Considering this, more buyers may enter if it touches $48,03 or the 0.618 point.

Conclusion

Papa John’s International, Inc. faces challenges in the domestic market. But its solid performance in franchising and international markets helps in its slight recovery and profitability. Its well-positioned balance sheet and business model promise liquidity and sustainability. What’s more interesting today is the potential buyout that may lead to at least 15% gains among investors. Its P/E and P/S valuation justify the offer price of $60. Yet, it still has some right to ask for a higher bid price. Technicals also support our optimistic view as bullish signals emerge. I'm reiterating my buy rating on this.

data.title: Can Private Markets Gain Traction In 401(k) Plans?
data.description:
By Fred Barstein

The new administration seems amenable toward private markets investments, which could help expand their presence in the defined contribution space.

By most accounts, the Trump administration and its Securities and Exchange Commission is taking a relatively friendly view of private markets, including cryptocurrency. As such, there is growing speculation that private market investments, especially private equity, could be made more available to DC plan participants. But with ERISA’s heightened focus on fees and transparency, will retirement plan advisors and sponsors take up the opportunity?

Examining the Case

It’s worth considering the merits of private markets investments and why they have been of interest to the DC world. Potential benefits include higher returns, reduced volatility and access to emerging opportunities.

A study conducted by the Georgetown University Center for Retirement Initiatives, in conjunction with CEM Benchmarking, tested three scenarios of adding illiquid assets to target date options from 2011 to 2020. Based on researchers' assumptions, the study found that DC plan participants may be missing out on up to $35 billion in annual investment growth due to a 0.15% lower return than defined benefit plans achieved with diversified portfolios that include private equity.1

Moreover, since private equity investments have relatively low correlations to public markets, they can help to mitigate market volatility.

In terms of opportunity, with fewer companies going public, DC participants currently have reduced access to growing companies that are available within the private equity space.

However, these advantages often come with trade-offs such as higher fees, limited liquidity, sometimes opaque valuation methods, complex reporting, participant confusion and less regulatory oversight, as outlined in a 2025 Bloomberg column.2 Morningstar also recently debated the pros and cons of private markets in DC plans.3

Is a Shift in Store?

Looking back, the first Trump administration took a relatively favorable stance toward including private equity in DC plans, while the Biden Department of Labor was more cautious. In a 2021 revised information letter,4 while not prohibiting the inclusion of private equity in DC plans, the Biden DOL emphasized the fiduciary duty to conduct thorough due diligence and evaluate such investments relative to other available options. Now, given the Trump administration’s past policies and more recent vocal support of cryptocurrency, it seems likely that private markets will see a more favorable regulatory recession.

As reflected in a recent session at the April 2025 NAPA Summit, “Alternative Realities: is the Time Ripe for Alternative Investments in DC Plans?”, interest in private markets among DC plans already seems to be growing.5

Even with uneven growth in the past, the amount of private equity in the retirement segment is substantial: as of 4Q 2024, $12.4 trillion was in DC plans, according to the ICI, with target date funds topping $4 trillion at the end of 1Q 2025.6 For the most part, additional private investments likely would not be standalone options on plan menus, but rather a sleeve in a target date fund or part of a managed account where the advisor has fiduciary responsibility for the asset allocation. The cap of 15% on illiquid assets allowed in target date mutual funds prompts some experts to anticipate a roughly 10% allocation for private equity within such funds.

Balancing the Variables

Should advisors recommend TDFs with allocations to private equity? One study by Neuberger Berman modeled two hypothetical TDF portfolios over a 40-year glide path, one with a 10% private equity allocation and one without.7 The results showed private equity improved wealth accumulation, often with only a slight increase in portfolio fees.

Of course, past performance does not indicate future returns, but with its higher return potential and lower volatility, we believe private equity presents a strong case—though advisors must consider suitability, particularly for those nearing retirement.

As with all investments, advisors need to understand the needs and situations of their clients to determine what investments should be offered, but the case for private markets is strong, with momentum building and perhaps enabling advisors to differentiate themselves.

1 Georgetown University Center for Retirement Initiatives, “Has the Lack of Asset Diversification in DC Retirement Plans Been a Costly Missed Opportunity?”, June 2023.

2 Bloomberg, Private Equity Does Not Belong in Your 401(K), January 2025.

3 Morningstar, Is Private Equity Coming to Your 401(k)?, April 2025.

4 U.S. Department of Labor, U.S. Department of Labor Supplement Statement on Private Equity in Defined Contribution Plan Designated Investment Alternatives, December 2021.

5 National Association of Plan Advisors, The Growing Case for Alternatives in Retirement Saving: NAPA 401(k) Summit, April 2025.

6 ICI, Quarterly Retirement Market Data, March 2025.

7 Neuberger Berman, Unlocked Potential: Enhancing DC Outcomes With Private Equity, January 2025.

FOR INVESTMENT PROFESSIONAL, BROKER-DEALER AND INSTITUTIONAL USE ONLY. NOT FOR USE WITH OR DISTRIBUTION TO THE GENERAL PUBLIC.

Notice of Non-Affiliation and Disclaimer: Fred Barstein, TRAU, TPSU and Editor in Chief of 401kTV, is not affiliated with Neuberger Berman. Use of these names does not imply any affiliation with or endorsement by them.

Third-party media content is being redistributed with permission and may not be redistributed without the publisher’s consent. This material is provided for informational purposes only and nothing herein constitutes investment, legal, accounting or tax advice, or a recommendation to buy, sell or hold a security. Any views or opinions expressed may not reflect those of the firm or the firm as a whole and Neuberger does not endorse any third-party views expressed. Neuberger Berman has not sought to independently verify any information in such third-party content and does not make any representation or warranty as to the accuracy, completeness or reliability of such information contain herein. Nothing herein implies any endorsement, approval, investigation or verification by Neuberger Berman of any third-party materials, products or services. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment or strategy is suitable for a particular investor. All information is current as of the date of this material and is subject to change without notice.
